matches.match_rationale: AI can generate exam questions aligned to specified learning outcomes, difficulty levels, and bloom's taxonomy tiers — directly countering the root cause of teachers creating questions in isolation without a standard. For a multi-site operator, this provides a repeatable, prompt-driven authoring tool that enforces quality parameters at the point of creation rather than after review.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A question-generation tool where teachers input the learning outcome, subject, grade, and desired difficulty; the AI produces draft items with distractors and mapped standards, inserted directly into a shared item bank.

matches.match_rationale: Existing exam questions can be classified by Bloom's taxonomy level, difficulty tier, and alignment to learning outcomes — providing the org its first structured view of what its question bank actually contains. This directly surfaces the inconsistency problem by labelling which items meet standards and which do not.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated item audit pipeline that ingests past exam papers and tags each question by cognitive level, topic, and outcome alignment, producing a quality dashboard per subject and site.

matches.match_rationale: The full exam quality workflow — question submission, automated classification, peer/AI review, approval, and item bank ingestion — is a multi-step process that currently relies on ad hoc teacher behaviour. A task automation agent can enforce the process: route new questions through classification and alignment checks, flag items that fail thresholds, and only release approved items to the shared bank.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An exam quality gate agent: teacher submits question → agent classifies and checks outcome alignment → flags or approves → approved items route to the centralised item bank with metadata attached.

matches.match_rationale: A RAG system over the organisation's curriculum documents and approved item bank allows teachers to query 'find me three difficulty-4 questions aligned to outcome X' and receive grounded, sourced answers — directly replacing the current practice of writing in isolation. It surfaces existing approved items before teachers write new ones, reducing duplication and drift.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A teacher-facing exam assistant: query the item bank by learning outcome and difficulty, retrieve existing approved questions, or generate new ones grounded in curriculum documents — all in one interface.
